svg.text: YEMENITE HEBREW
PHONOLOGY — MOST CONSERVATIVE TRADITION
YEMENITE: PRESERVING ANCIENT SOUNDS
Yemenite pronunciation: most conservative Hebrew tradition surviving
Preserves distinctions lost in Ashkenazi, Sephardic, and Modern Hebrew
YEMENITE UNIQUE CONSONANTS
Gimel with dagesh: /g/ • Gimel without: /ɣ/ (voiced velar fricative)
Dalet with dagesh: /d/ • Dalet without: /ð/ (voiced interdental)
Tav with dagesh: /t/ • Tav without: /θ/ (voiceless interdental)
These fricatives: lost in most traditions, fully preserved in Yemenite
AYIN DISTINCTION
Ayin /ʿ/: voiced pharyngeal fricative (like Arabic ayin)
Yemenite: full pharyngeal — sounds identical to Arabic /ʿ/
Ashkenazi/Modern: ayin = silent (merged with aleph)
Yemenite ayin sounds: more Arabic than other Hebrew traditions
QAMATZ DISTINCTION
Qamatz gadol: /ɑ/ (back a) • Qamatz qatan: /o/ (Yemenite distinguishes!)
Most traditions: both qamatz = same vowel
Yemenite: keeps them distinct as in original Masoretic intention
YEMENI ARABIC PHONOLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
YEMENI ARABIC CONSERVATISM
Yemeni Arabic preserves /q/ as uvular stop (Classical value)
Preserves /θ/ (thalatha "three") and /ð/ (hadhihi "this") as interdentals
Most Arabic dialects: /θ/ → /t/ or /s/ • Yemeni: keeps original
PARALLEL CONSERVATISM
Yemenite Hebrew and Yemeni Arabic: both highly conservative
Both preserve interdental sounds (/θ/ /ð/) lost in most sister forms
Yemenite Hebrew ayin /ʿ/ = Yemeni Arabic ayin /ʿ/ (same phoneme!)
Geographic isolation of Yemen preserved ancient features in both languages
PLATE 11b ALT · YEMENITE HEBREW / ARABIC · PHONOLOGY
